content: Lenz's law of electromagnetic induction corresponds to the

options.content: law of conservation of angular momentum

explanation:
Lenz's law corresponds to the law of conservation of energy.

Lenz's law states that the direction of an induced current is always such as to oppose the change in the magnetic flux that produces it. This means that the induced current will create a magnetic field that opposes the change in the original magnetic field. This opposition to change is what conserves energy.

For example, if a magnet is moved towards a coil of wire, the magnetic flux through the coil will increase. This will induce a current in the coil, which will create a magnetic field that opposes the motion of the magnet. This opposition to motion is what conserves energy.

The other options are incorrect.

Option A, the law of conservation of charge, is not related to Lenz's law.

Option B, the law of conservation of momentum, is related to Newton's third law of motion.

Option C, the law of conservation of angular momentum, is related to rotational motion.

content: Two straight conducting plates form an angle $$\theta$$ where their ends are joined. A conducting bar in contact with the plates and forming an isosceles triangle with them starts at the vertex at time $$t=0$$ and moves with constant velocity $$\vec{v}$$ to the right as shown in figure. A magnetic field $$\vec{B}$$ points out of the page. The magnitude of emf induced at $$t=1$$ second will be
explanation:
Area $=\frac{1}{2}\left(2 x \tan \frac{\theta}{2}\right) x$

$\begin{aligned} & \text { flux, } \varphi=\left(x^2 \tan \frac{\theta}{2}\right) B \\\\ & \frac{d \varphi}{d t}=\left(2 x \frac{d x}{d t} \tan \frac{\theta}{2}\right) B=2 x v B \tan \frac{\theta}{2}=2(v t) v B \tan \frac{\theta}{2} \\\\ & \text { At } t=1 \mathrm{sec}, \quad \varepsilon=2 v^2 B \tan \frac{\theta}{2}\end{aligned}$

content: The electric field of a plane electromagnetic wave of wave number k and angular frequency $$\omega$$ is given $$\vec{E}=E_{0}(\hat{i}+\hat{j}) \sin (k z-\omega t)$$. Which of the following gives the direction of the associated magnetic field $$\vec{B}$$ ?
explanation:
The direction of the magnetic field in an electromagnetic wave can be determined using the cross product of the wave vector and the electric field vector. In this case, the electric field is given by $\vec{E} = E_0(\hat{\mathbf{i}} + \hat{\mathbf{j}}) \sin(kz - \omega t)$, and the wave is propagating in the $+\hat{\mathbf{k}}$ direction (along the z-axis).

The direction of the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ can be found using the right-hand rule for the cross product $\hat{\mathbf{v}} \times \vec{E}$, where $\hat{\mathbf{v}}$ is the direction of wave propagation. In this case, $\hat{\mathbf{v}} = \hat{\mathbf{k}}$. So, we calculate :

$ \hat{\mathbf{v}} \times \vec{E} = \hat{\mathbf{k}} \times (\hat{\mathbf{i}} + \hat{\mathbf{j}}) $

This cross product gives us the direction of the magnetic field :

  • The cross product $\hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \hat{\mathbf{i}}$ yields $+\hat{\mathbf{j}}$.
  • The cross product $\hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \hat{\mathbf{j}}$ yields $-\hat{\mathbf{i}}$.

So, combining these results, we get :

$ \hat{\mathbf{k}} \times (\hat{\mathbf{i}} + \hat{\mathbf{j}}) = -\hat{\mathbf{j}} + \hat{\mathbf{i}} = -\hat{\mathbf{i}} + \hat{\mathbf{j}} $

Therefore, the direction of the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ associated with the electromagnetic wave is $-\hat{\mathbf{i}} + \hat{\mathbf{j}}$, which corresponds to Option B.

content: A charged particle in a uniform magnetic field $$\vec{B}=B_{0} \hat{k}$$ starts moving from the origin with velocity $$v=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}} ~\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$$. The trajectory of the particle and the time $$t$$ at which it reaches $$2 \mathrm{~m}$$ above $$\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}$$ plane are,
explanation:
As the component of velocity is along $$\vec{B}$$ as well as perpendicular to $$\vec{B}$$. Hence, the trajectory of the particle is helical path.

Also,

$$\begin{aligned} & v_z=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, z=2 \mathrm{~m} \\ &\therefore \quad z=v_z \cdot {t} \\ & \therefore \quad t=\frac{z}{v_z}=\frac{2}{4}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~s} \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: In a closed circuit there is only a coil of inductance L and resistance 100 $$\Omega$$. The coil is situated in a uniform magnetic field. All on a sudden, the magnetic flux linked with the circuit changes by 5 Weber. What amount of charge will flow in the circuit as a result?

options.content: Value of L is to be known to find the charge flown

explanation:
Given, change in flux, $$d\phi = 5$$ Wb

Emf due to change in flux is given as

$$E = {{d\phi } \over {dt}} \Rightarrow E\,.\,dt = 5$$

Now, it is given that, $$R = 100\,\Omega $$

Thus, current, $$I = {V \over R} = {E \over R}$$ ..... (i)

Also, $$I = {{dq} \over {dt}}$$ ....... (ii)

where, $$dq$$ is the charge flowing per unit time.

Thus, from Eqs. (i) and (ii), we have

$${E \over R} = {{dq} \over {dt}} \Rightarrow {{E\,.\,dt} \over R} = dq$$

$$ \Rightarrow {5 \over {100}} = dq \Rightarrow dq = 0.05\,C$$

content:
For a plane electromagnetic wave, the electric field is given by

$$ \overrightarrow{E} = 90\sin (0.5 \times {10^3}x + 1.5 \times {10^{11}}t)\widehat k$$ V/m. The corresponding magnetic field B will be
explanation:
For a plane electromagnetic wave, the electric field is given by

$$E = 90\sin (0.5 \times {10^3}x + 1.5 \times {10^{11}}t)\widehat k$$ V/m

Here, E0 = 90 V/m

$$\therefore$$ $${B_0} = {{{E_0}} \over c}$$

$$ = {{90} \over {3 \times {{10}^8}}}$$

= 30 $$\times$$ 10$$-$$8 T

= 3 $$\times$$ 10$$-$$7 T

Since, electric field vector and magnetic field vector, both are perpendicular to each other and also perpendicular to direction of propagation of electromagnetic wave. Hence,

$$B = {B_0}\sin (0.5 \times {10^3}x + 1.5 \times {10^{11}}t)\widehat j$$ T

$$B = 3 \times {10^{ - 7}}\sin (0.5 \times {10^3}x + 1.5 \times {10^{11}}t)\widehat j$$ T

content: Consider a conducting wire of length L bent in the form of a circle of radius R and another conductor of length a (a < < R) is bent in the form of a square. The two loops are then placed in same plane such that the square loop is exactly at the centre of the circular loop. What will be the mutual inductance between the two loops?
explanation:
A conducting wire of length L bent in the form of circle of radius R and another conductor of length a is bent in the form of a square. The two loops are then placed in same plane such that the square loop is exactly at the centre of circular loop as shown in the figure

For circular loop,

2$$\pi $$R = L ............(i)

For square of side S,

4S = a .............(ii)

Flux linked, $$\phi = {{{\mu _0}I{S^2}} \over {2R}}$$

Mutual inductance, $$M{\phi \over I} = {{{\mu _0}{S^2}} \over {2R}}$$ Substituting R from Eq. (i) and S from Eq. (ii), we get

$$M = {{{\mu _0}{a^2}} \over {2(16)}}{{2\pi } \over L} = {{{\mu _0}{a^2}\pi } \over {16L}}$$

content:
A conducting circular loop of resistance 20$$\Omega $$
 and cross-sectional area 20 $$ \times $$ 10<sup>-2</sup> m<sup>2</sup> is placed perpendicular to a spatially uniform magnetic field B, which varies with time t as B = 2sin(50$$\pi $$t) T. Find the net charge flowing through the loop in 20 ms starting from t = 0.
explanation:
Given, resistance of the circular loop = 20$$\Omega $$

Electric charge flowing due to the induced current,

$$q = I.dt = {{\Delta \phi } \over R} = {{{\phi _2} - {\phi _1}} \over R}$$ ,

where $${{\phi _1}}$$ , $${{\phi _2}}$$ are magnetic flux at t1 = 0 and t2 = 20 ms.

As the loop is perpendicular to the magnetic field B, the flux passing through the loop is,

$${\phi _1} = B.A = BA\cos 90^\circ = 0$$

Hence, $$q = {{\Delta \phi } \over R} = {{{\phi _2} - {\phi _1}} \over R} = 0$$

$$ \therefore $$ q = 0 C

content: As shown in the figure, a rectangular loop of conducting wire is moving away with a constant velocity v in a perpendicular direction from a very long straight conductor carrying a steady current I. When the breadth of the rectangular loop is very small compared to its distance from the straight conductor, how does the emf. E induced in the loop vary with time t?
explanation:
$$ \therefore $$ Motional emf in the loop,

$$E = {E_1} - {E_2} = vl({B_1} - {B_2})$$

$$ = vl\left[ {{{{\mu _0}} \over {2\pi }}.{i \over y} - {{{\mu _0}I} \over {2\pi (y + b)}}} \right]$$

$$ = {{{\mu _0}} \over {2\pi }}.I.vl\left[ {{{y + b - y} \over {y(y + b)}}} \right] = {{{\mu _0}} \over {2\pi }}.{{vlI} \over {y(y + b)}}b$$

$$ \because $$ $$b < < y$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$E = {{{\mu _0}} \over {2\pi }}.{{vlI} \over {{y^2}}}b$$

$$ \because $$ v = constant ($$ \therefore $$ y = vt)

Thus, $$E = {{{\mu _0}} \over {2\pi }}.{{vlI} \over {{v^2}{t^2}}}b$$

Hence, $$E \propto {1 \over {{t^2}}}$$

content: Two coils of self-inductances 6 mH and 8 mH are connected in series and are adjusted for highest coefficient of coupling. Equivalent self-inductance L for the assembly is approximately
explanation:
Given, L1 = 6 mH = 6 $$ \times $$ 10$$-$$3 H

L2 = 8 mH = 8 $$ \times $$ 10$$-$$3 H

If two coils L1 and L2 are connected in series, the equivalent self-inductance L for assembly

$$L = {L_1} + {L_2} + 2\sqrt {{L_1}{L_2}} $$

$$ = 6 \times {10^{ - 3}} + 8 \times {10^{ - 3}} + 2\sqrt {6 \times 8 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} $$

$$ = 14 \times {10^{ - 3}} + 2\sqrt {48} \times {10^{ - 3}}$$

$$ = 14 \times {10^{ - 3}} + 2 \times 7 \times {10^{ - 3}}$$ ($$ \because $$ $$\sqrt {48} = 6.92 = 7$$)

$$ = 28 \times {10^{ - 3}}$$ H = 28 mH

content: A circular coil is placed near a current carrying conductor, both lying on the plane of the paper. The current is flowing through the conductor in such a way that the induced current in the loop is clockwise as shown in the figure. The current in the wire is,
explanation:
If current is increasing in upward direction, so magnetic field is increasing in out of plane in order to oppose it induced current will be in clockwise direction



Similarly if current is decreasing in downward direction, so magnetic field is decreasing into the plane in order to support it induced current will be in clockwise direction. So both option A and C can be correct.

content: A charged particle of charge q and mass m is placed at a distance 2R from the centre of a vertical cylindrical region of radius R where magnetic field varies as $$\vec{B}=\left(4 t^{2}-2 t+6\right) \hat{k}$$, where t is time. Then which of the following statements is/are true?

options.content: Induced electric field lines form closed loops.
Electric field varies linearly with r if r < R, where r is the radial distance from the centerline of the cylinder.
The charged particle will move in clockwise direction when viewed from top.
Acceleration of the charged particle is $$\mathrm{\frac{7q}{2m}}$$ when t = 2 sec.

explanation:
$$\vec{B}=\left(4 t^2-2 t+6\right) \hat{\mathbf{k}} \Rightarrow \frac{d B}{d t}=(8 t-2)$$

By Maxwell's equation,

For $$r < R$$,

$$\begin{aligned} & & \oint E_{\text {ind }} \cdot d l & =\frac{d \phi_B}{d t}=-\left(\frac{d B}{d t}\right) A \\ \Rightarrow & & E_{\text {ind }}(2 \pi r) & =-(8 t-2) \cdot \pi R^2 \\ \therefore & & E_{\text {ind }} & =-(4 t-1) \cdot \frac{R^2}{r} \end{aligned}$$

$$\Rightarrow E_{\text {ind }} \propto \frac{1}{r} \Rightarrow E_{\text {ind }}$$ vs $$r$$ does not vary linearly.

As $$E_{\text {ind }}$$ is a non-conservative field. So, it will form a closed loop.

Now, $$\left(\frac{d B}{d t}\right)_{t=0}=8 \times 0-2=-2$$

$$\therefore B$$ will decrease. So, by Lenz's law the charge particle will move in anti-clockwise direction to oppose the decreasing magnetic field.

$$\text { Now, }\left|E_{\text {ind }}\right|_{t=2 \mathrm{~s}}=\left.(4 t-1) \cdot \frac{R^2}{2 R}\right|_{t=2}=\frac{7 R}{2}$$

$$\therefore$$ Acceleration of charge particle is

$$a=\frac{q E_{\text {ind }}}{m}=\frac{q}{m} \cdot \frac{7 R}{2}=\frac{7 q R}{2 m}$$

content:
A particle of mass m and charge q moving with velocity v enters region-b from region-a along the normal to the boundary as shown in the figure. Region-b has a uniform magnetic field B perpendicular to the plane of the paper. Also, region-b has length L.

Choose the correct statement.

options.content: The particle enters region-c only if $$v > {{qLB} \over m}$$.
The particle enters region-c only if $$v < {{qLB} \over m}$$.
Path of the particle is a circle in region-b.
Time spent in region-b is independent of velocity v.

explanation:
The given situation is shown below


Since, charge particle enters into magnetic field B perpendicular to it, hence it performs a circular path in magnetic field. Radius of circular path,

$$r = {{mv} \over {Bq}}$$

Charge particle will enter in region c, when L < 2r

$$ \Rightarrow L < 2{{mv} \over {Bq}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{LBq} \over {2m}} < v \Rightarrow v > {{qLB} \over {2m}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow v > \left( {{1 \over 2}} \right){{qLB} \over m}$$

i.e. When $$v > {{qLB} \over m}$$, then particle surely will inter into region c, because $${{qLB} \over m} > {{qLB} \over {2m}}$$

Hence, option (a) is correct.

Since, charge particle enters into region c, hence path of the particle is a circle not in region b.

Hence, option (c) is not correct.

Time spent in region b is given as $$T = {{2\pi m} \over {Bq}}$$, which is independent of v. Hence, option (d) is correct.
